content.problemStatement:
You are given two sets of points SSS and TTT on the xyxyxy-plane. SSS contains NNN points and TTT contains  MMM points.

The points (xi,yi)(x_i, y_i)(xi​,yi​) in TTT have a special property - for every 1≤i<M1\leq i \lt M1≤i<M, xi+1>xix_{i+1} \gt x_ixi+1​>xi​ and yi+1<yiy_{i+1} \lt y_iyi+1​<yi​.

For each point (x,y)∈T(x, y) \in T(x,y)∈T, find the minimum Manhattan distance between (x,y)(x, y)(x,y) and a point in SSS. Formally, for each (x,y)∈T(x, y) \in T(x,y)∈T, find

min⁡(xi,yi)∈S(∣x−xi∣+∣y−yi∣)\min_{(x_i, y_i) \in S}(|x - x_i| + |y - y_i|)min(xi​,yi​)∈S​(∣x−xi​∣+∣y−yi​∣)


Input Format

The first line of input contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM - the size of SSS and TTT, respectively.
The next NNN lines each contain two space-separated integers xix_ixi​ and yiy_iyi​, representing the points of SSS.
The last MMM lines each contain two space-separated integers xix_ixi​ and yiy_iyi​, representing the points of TTT.


Output Format

For each test case, output MMM lines. The iii-th of these lines should contain a single integer, the minimum Manhattan distance between the iii-th point in TTT and a point in set SSS.
constraints: The points of T satisfy x1 < x2 < … < xM and y1 > y2 > … > yM
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains two integers N and M – the sizes of sets S and T. The next N lines each contain two integers xi yi, the coordinates of a point in S. The following M lines each contain two integers xj yj, the coordinates of a point in T. The points of T are given in strictly increasing order of x and strictly decreasing order of y (i.e., x1 < x2 < … < xM and y1 > y2 > … > yM).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output M lines. The i‑th line contains a single integer – the minimum Manhattan distance between the i‑th point of T and any point of S.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
The sets are S={(0,0),(1,1)}S = \{(0, 0), (1, 1)\}S={(0,0),(1,1)} and T={(0,1),(1,0)}T = \{(0, 1), (1, 0)\}T={(0,1),(1,0)}. Every point in SSS is at a (Manhattan) distance of 111 from every point in TTT.

Explanation:
The sets are S={(2,2),(3,3),(3,1)}S = \{(2, 2), (3, 3), (3, 1)\}S={(2,2),(3,3),(3,1)} and T={(0,3),(3,1),(5,0)}T = \{(0, 3), (3, 1), (5, 0)\}T={(0,3),(3,1),(5,0)}.

The point closest to (0,3)(0, 3)(0,3) is (3,3)(3, 3)(3,3), with distance 333
The point (3,1)(3, 1)(3,1) in SSS coincides with the point (3,1)(3, 1)(3,1) in set TTT.
The point closest to (5,0)(5, 0)(5,0) is (3,1)(3, 1)(3,1), with distance 333


The points in red are the points of the set SSS and those in blue belong to set TTT. The green-coloured line segments represent the Manhattan distance between two points.

solution.approach: For a point (x, y) the Manhattan distance to (xi, yi) can be written as:  d = max(|(xi+yi) - (x+y)|, |(xi-yi) - (x-y)|).  If we define Ai = xi+yi and Bi = xi-yi, the problem becomes: given a set of points (Ai, Bi), for each query (A = x+y, B = x-y) find the minimum Chebyshev distance max(|Ai-A|,|Bi-B|).  This is equivalent to finding the smallest d such that there exists a point with Ai in [A-d, A+d] and Bi in [B-d, B+d].  We pre‑process the points of S: 1) compute (Ai, Bi) for every point, 2) sort them by Ai and build a merge‑sort segment tree where each node stores the sorted list of Bi values of its interval.  For a fixed d we can query the tree in O(log N) nodes; each node is checked with a binary search on its Bi list to see whether any Bi lies in [B-d, B+d].  Using this predicate we binary‑search d for each query.  The coordinate range is at most 2·10^9, so 31 iterations of the outer binary search are enough.  Overall complexity per query is O(log C · log N) (C – coordinate range), i.e. about 31·log N, which easily fits the limits.  The total time is O((N+M)·log N·log C) and the memory consumption is O(N·log N) for the merge‑sort tree.
solution.timeComplexity: O((N + M) · log N · log C) where C ≤ 2·10^9 (≈31 bits).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N · log N) for the merge‑sort segment tree.
